data.title: McEwen Mining to acquire Golden Lake Exploration in all-share deal
data.description:
• McEwen Mining (MUX) and Golden Lake Exploration (GOLXF) have entered into a definitive agreement under which McEwen will acquire all issued and outstanding shares of Golden Lake through a court-approved plan of arrangement, consolidating Golden Lake’s assets into McEwen’s growing portfolio.
  • If the proposed transaction is completed, Golden Lake would become a wholly owned subsidiary of McEwen.
  • Under the proposed transaction, Golden Lake shareholders would receive 0.003876 McEwen shares for each Golden Lake share.
  • The exchange ratio represents an implied offer price of C$0.12 per Golden Lake share, being a premium of 60% to the 20-day volume-weighted average price of the Golden Lake shares as at market close on January 26, 2026.
  • Golden Lake’s principal asset is its 100%-owned Jewel Ridge and Jewel Ridge West projects located adjacent to McEwen’s (MUX) Windfall and Lookout Mountain discoveries, part of the Gold Bar Mine Complex, in the Eureka Mining District of Nevada.

data.title: Samsung Electronics GAAP EPS of KRW 2909.00, revenue of KRW 93.8B
data.description:
• Samsung Electronics press release (SSNLF): Q4 GAAP EPS of KRW 2,909.00.
  • Revenue of KRW 93.8T (+23.7% Y/Y).
  • Operating profit: 20.1 trillion won.

  • “Looking ahead to Q1 2026, the DS Division expects AI and server demand to continue increasing, leading to more opportunities for structural growth,” the company said.

    “In response, the Division will continue to focus on profitability via a strong emphasis on high-performance products,” it added.

data.title: Bumble, Match, Panera Bread, and CrunchBase hit by cyberattacks, Bloomberg reports
data.description:
A wave of cyberattacks has hit Bumble (BMBL), Panera Bread (PANERA), Match Group (MTCH), and CrunchBase, as cybersecurity experts warn about a new round of social engineering attacks targeting US companies, Bloomberg News reported.

Bumble, the parent company of dating apps Bumble, Badoo, and BFF, contacted law enforcement after one of its contractor’s accounts “was recently compromised in a phishing incident,” the report said, citing a spokesperson.

Bumble said the intruders did not get into the member database, accounts, direct messages, or profiles, while Tinder parent Match said an incident affected a limited amount of user data, but there was no indication that user log-in credentials, financial information, or private communications were accessed, according to the Bloomberg report.

data.title: Big tech eyes $60B bet on OpenAI as Nvidia, Amazon, Microsoft enter talks: report
data.description:
• Nvidia (NVDA), Amazon (AMZN), and Microsoft (MSFT) are reportedly in talks to invest up to $60 billion ‌in OpenAI (OPENAI).
  • Nvidia (NVDA), an existing investor whose chips power OpenAI's AI models, is in talks to invest up to $30 billion, The Information reported on Wednesday.
  • OpenAI is close to receiving ⁠term sheets, or an investment commitment, from these ‍firms, the report said.
  • Amazon's (AMZN) investment could depend on separate negotiations, including a possible expansion of OpenAI's cloud ⁠server rental deal with ⁠Amazon and ​a commercial agreement for OpenAI to sell its products, such as enterprise ChatGPT subscriptions, to Amazon, The Information said.
  • This development follows reports from ‍earlier this week that said that SoftBank (SFTBY) is in talks to invest as much as an additional $30 billion in OpenAI.

data.title: C.H. Robinson targets double-digit productivity improvements in 2026 through Lean AI expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc. (CHRW) Q4 2025

Management View

  • David Bozeman, President and CEO, opened by highlighting, “The fourth quarter certainly provided a challenging macro environment with weak global freight demand, rising spot costs in trucking and falling ocean rates all providing headwinds to our business.” He emphasized that despite these pressures, “we grew our total volume by 1% and our truckload volume by approximately 3% on a year-over-year basis compared to a 7.6% year-over-year decline in the Cass Freight Shipment Index. This reflects another quarter of demonstrable market share growth.”
  • Bozeman stressed the ongoing transformation, stating, “We are using our trusted domain expertise, to build technology that delivers on our customer promise and drives higher value for all of our stakeholders.” He underscored the company’s commitment to scaling Lean AI initiatives, noting, “Significant runway exists as we continue to deepen the Lean mindset and scale custom-built AI agents across the enterprise.”
  • Damon Lee, Chief Financial Officer, stated, “Q4 personnel expenses were $337 million, including $15.2 million of restructuring charges related to workforce reductions. Excluding restructuring charges in 2025 and 2024, our Q4 personnel expenses were $321.8 million, down $28.8 million or 8.2% due to our continued productivity and cost optimization efforts and the divestiture of our Europe Surface Transportation business.” Lee added, “As a result of our efforts to grow market share, improve gross margins and increase our productivity and operating leverage, we expanded our operating margin, excluding restructuring costs, by 320 basis points year-over-year.”
  • Michael Castagnetto, President of North American Surface Transportation, detailed market share gains, saying, “For the 11th consecutive quarter, the team delivered market share growth in Q4... our combined truckload and LTL volume delivered positive growth of approximately 1% year-over-year. Truckload volume rose approximately 3% and LTL volume increased approximately 0.5% year-over-year.”
  • Arun Rajan, Chief Strategy & Innovation Officer, described the technology strategy: “We own and control the code and we own the application layer because we are building our own AI agents. We, therefore, have more control over the implementation process and the speed of integrating those agents with our proprietary technology landscape.”

Outlook

  • Lee indicated, “We expect 2026 personnel expenses to be in the guidance range of $1.25 billion to $1.35 billion. This includes an expectation that we will generate double-digit productivity improvements in both NAST and Global Forwarding in 2026 as we continue to implement agentic AI across our quote-to-cash life cycle of an order.”
  • SG&A expenses for 2026 are expected to be $540 million to $590 million, with depreciation and amortization of $95 million to $105 million for the year.
  • For 2026, the full year tax rate is forecasted to range from 18% to 20%. Capital expenditures are guided at $75 million to $85 million for the year.
  • Lee noted, “On a quarterly basis, it's important to note that our Q1 personnel expenses are expected to increase sequentially due to the employer portion of FICA taxes resetting to a higher level... This impact is estimated to be approximately $15 million in Q1 versus Q4.”

Financial Results

  • Lee reported, “Q4 total revenue in AGP declined approximately 7% and 4% year-over-year, respectively. The AGP decline was driven by a 13% year-over-year decline in Global Forwarding AGP, which was primarily due to a significant drop in ocean rates... partially offset by a 2% increase in NAST AGP.”
  • The company generated $305.4 million in cash from operations in Q4 and ended the quarter with approximately $1.49 billion of liquidity.
  • Net debt-to-EBITDA leverage at the end of Q4 was 1.03x, down from 1.17x at the end of Q3.
  • The company returned approximately $207.7 million of cash to shareholders in Q4 through $133.3 million of share repurchases and $74.3 million of dividends.

Q&A

  • Thomas Wadewitz, UBS: Asked about adjusted gross profit growth in Q1 and longer-term margin potential. Lee responded, “December was heavily impacted by global forwarding and the ocean rate normalization... we expect that to continue into Q1 as well.” Regarding margins, “We're still on a really good trajectory to get to those mid-cycle margins that we laid out for NAST and Global Forwarding with 40% being the margin target for NAST at mid-cycle.”
  • Bascome Majors, Susquehanna: Inquired about balancing margin expansion and share growth in 2026. Lee answered, “We want to make the right decision for what I consider quality earnings growth. Once we get to what we consider is our threshold for quality of earnings, which we've set at already, right? 40% for NAST at mid-cycle and 30% for Global Forwarding... we'll make the decision beyond that point on what is the right decision for earnings growth.”
  • Brandon Oglenski, Barclays: Questioned the Lean operating model and AI deployment. Bozeman explained the company’s approach is driven by “our people... our technology, which we think is industry leading... and then three is our Lean operating model in which that is the motor that brings all of this together.” Rajan added, “Our engineers have been at this company for a long time, and they understand this industry really well. So in terms of speed... we get incredible speed with this builder culture.”
  • Reed Seay, Stephens: Asked about headcount reductions. Bozeman stated, “We engineer the business... we're shifting to more customer focus... we're automating that. And for some of that, we're not backfilling.” Lee added, “The process itself has fundamentally changed. The technology allows us to scale at a very large magnitude.”
  • Scott Group, Wolfe Research: Queried about market share growth and personnel expense guidance. Lee clarified, “We are committed with high confidence to the $6 EPS target that we updated in our Q3 earnings call, again, with a very high confidence.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, pressing for clarity on margin sustainability, headcount strategy, and the scalability of technology. Questions were direct, often focusing on the durability of outperformance and margin targets.
  • Management’s tone was confident and occasionally assertive, especially when explaining competitive advantages and Lean AI initiatives, as seen in Lee’s “We feel confident we'll continue to overdeliver and execute our strategy in Q1.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management sustained a similar tone—analysts remained cautious yet optimistic, while management consistently projected confidence in operational discipline, technology, and future targets.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q4’s market commentary cited a continuation of challenging macro conditions, with emphasis shifting to ocean rate declines and spot trucking cost spikes. The company reported a slight decrease in combined truckload and LTL volume growth (1% vs. 3% in Q3), but highlighted ongoing market share gains.
  • Management reaffirmed double-digit productivity improvement targets and $6 EPS guidance for 2026, first introduced in Q3.
  • Strategic focus continues to center on Lean AI and automation, with no major changes in strategic direction versus Q3.
  • Analysts’ questions remained focused on margin durability, technology differentiation, and growth optionality, echoing previous quarter themes.
  • Management’s sentiment of discipline, speed, and innovation remained consistent, with a continued emphasis on scalable technology and operating leverage.

Risks and Concerns

  • Macro headwinds in global freight demand, volatile spot market costs, and declining ocean rates were cited as ongoing challenges.
  • The company acknowledged the impact of regulatory changes on trucking capacity and the unpredictability of global trade policies.
  • Lee noted restructuring charges related to workforce reductions as part of cost optimization efforts.
  • Management’s mitigation focuses on Lean AI-driven productivity, cost discipline, diversified carrier networks, and ongoing automation.

Final Takeaway

C.H. Robinson closed 2025 by demonstrating continued market share gains, disciplined cost control, and successful execution of its Lean AI transformation despite persistent macro headwinds. Management remains confident in its ability to deliver double-digit productivity gains in 2026 and maintain its $6 EPS target, supported by scalable technology, operating leverage, and a robust balance sheet. The company’s focus on automation, data-driven decision-making, and strategic flexibility positions it to outperform across market cycles while maintaining industry-leading margins and shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Levi Strauss projects 4%-5% organic net revenue growth for 2026 as DTC momentum accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Levi Strauss & Co. (LEVI) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Michelle Gass, CEO, opened by stating the fourth quarter capped a strong year and highlighted, "In 2025, we delivered organic net revenue growth of 7%, which was broad-based across all facets of our business." She emphasized the company's focus on becoming a DTC-first, head-to-toe denim lifestyle retailer and noted, "The Levi's brand grew 7% and Levi's strengthened its standing as the #1 denim brand in the world and today, holds more market share than the next 2 global competitors combined."
  • Gass pointed to the accelerated DTC transformation, stating, "We accelerated our DTC transformation, growing 11%, which now comprises approximately half of our total business." She also cited a robust product pipeline and the completion of the global rollout of the Blue Tab premium collection.
  • On product momentum, Gass said, "In Q4, tops grew double digits, driving nearly half of our revenue growth and meaningfully higher AURs versus last year."
  • Harmit Singh, Chief Financial & Growth Officer, stated, "2025 was a strong year with continued consistent profitable growth for our company. I am pleased that our growth has accelerated over the last 3 years and we have established ourselves as a consistent mid-single-digit growth company, which we expect to continue in 2026." Singh highlighted the company's record gross margin and adjusted EBIT margin expansion, and noted a focus on mitigating tariff impacts and driving toward becoming a $10 billion DTC-first company.
  • Singh reported, "Organic net revenues grew 5% and were up 13% on a 2-year stack. Our outperformance was driven by better-than-expected results across channels and geographies." He also announced a $200 million ASR program to be completed within 3 to 6 months.

Outlook

  • Singh provided guidance for 2026, stating, "We expect organic net revenue growth of 4% to 5% with 1 point favorability from foreign exchange, resulting in reported net revenue growth of 5% to 6%." He outlined growth expectations by segment: Americas low single digits, Europe mid-single digits, and Asia mid- to high single digits.
  • Singh said, "By channel, we expect DTC to grow high single digits, fueled by positive comp sales opening 50 to 60 net new system doors and continued growth in e-commerce."
  • Gross margin is expected to be flat to prior year, with tariff impacts offset by pricing actions, higher full-price selling, product cost reduction, and SKU rationalization. Full year adjusted diluted EPS is expected to be in the range of $1.40 to $1.46.

Financial Results

  • Singh reported, "Gross margin for the quarter was 60.8% of net revenues, contracting 100 basis points relative to last year, in line with our expectations, primarily due to the impacts of tariffs, which were partially offset by pricing actions and higher full-price selling."
  • Adjusted EBIT margin contracted 180 basis points to 12.1%. Fourth quarter adjusted diluted EPS was $0.41, which includes a $0.03 headwind from a higher tax rate.
  • Inventory dollars were up 9% to prior year, "primarily driven by tariffs." The year ended with $363 million returned to shareholders, a 26% increase versus prior year.
  • Americas net revenues were up 2% for Q4. Europe net revenues accelerated, up 10%. Asia net revenues grew 4%. Beyond Yoga was up 45% in Q4 and ended the year up 17%.

Q&A

  • Laurent Vasilescu, BNP Paribas, asked about gross margins and drivers for 2026. Singh responded that "our guidance is, at this time, flat to prior year. What we have done nicely is offset the full impact of tariffs as the year progresses." He cited pricing, full-price selling, and lower product costs as mitigation factors.
  • Matthew Boss, JPMorgan, asked about the organic growth outlook and category leadership. Gass responded, "We feel really good heading into 2026...we are in the best shape that we've been in decades, both operationally and financially." Singh added that holiday sales were strong and confidence continues into Q1.
  • Jay Sole, UBS, inquired about DTC margin potential. Gass stated, "We absolutely believe that there's a lot of upside in DTC, both from a revenue standpoint and margin...margin growth will come from leverage."
  • Robert Drbul, BTIG, asked about Europe and Blue Tab performance. Singh highlighted double-digit growth in key markets and operating margin expansion. Gass said, "Blue Tab is clearly a global opportunity for us...early signs for Blue Tab are very positive."
  • Gabriella Garr, TD Cowen, asked about supply chain improvements. Gass said lead times have been reduced and SKU rationalization continues. Singh noted the U.S. distribution transition is ongoing, with benefits expected in the second half of 2026.
  • Additional analyst questions focused on pricing actions, wholesale rationalization, and SG&A leverage, with management reiterating confidence in margin expansion through operational discipline and AI-driven efficiencies.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts asked about gross margin conservatism, DTC opportunities, supply chain delays, and regional growth, showing a neutral to slightly positive but cautious tone. No significant skepticism or pressing negativity emerged; most queries centered on execution and margin drivers.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone, frequently referencing strong momentum, operational improvements, and successful transformation. Gass stated, "We are energized by the runway ahead and confident in our ability to keep driving the Levi's brand forward."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, sentiment remained positive, with a slightly greater emphasis on operational discipline, cost control, and mitigation of external headwinds.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 is more detailed, with explicit projections for organic net revenue growth (4%-5%) and EPS ($1.40 to $1.46). Previous quarter focused on raising full-year guidance for 2025 and described high single-digit growth trends.
  • Strategic priorities remain consistent: DTC acceleration, brand-led innovation, and international expansion. The current quarter places greater focus on margin expansion through AI-driven efficiencies and cost discipline.
  • Management confidence is strong in both quarters, though the current quarter underscores progress in managing tariffs and operational transitions. Analyst tone is steady, seeking more granularity on execution and mitigation strategies.
  • Key metrics such as gross margin, EBIT margin, and DTC growth are compared with prior results, highlighting the company's ability to sustain growth amid external challenges.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management pointed to ongoing tariff impacts, mitigated through pricing, cost reduction, and rationalization. Singh noted, "We are making meaningful progress on mitigating tariff impacts on our P&L through targeted pricing actions...and prudent management of our cost base."
  • The U.S. distribution transition is taking longer than expected, resulting in higher transitional costs, but management expects completion in 2026.
  • Analysts raised questions on wholesale rationalization, SG&A leverage, and supply chain lead times, with management providing detailed mitigation strategies and expressing confidence in execution.

Final Takeaway

Levi Strauss & Co. closed 2025 with broad-based revenue growth, strengthened DTC performance, and expanding product categories. Management projects continued mid-single-digit organic growth and flat gross margins for 2026, with targeted actions to offset tariffs and drive margin expansion. The company’s confidence is underscored by strong brand positioning, operational improvements, and a robust product pipeline, positioning Levi’s for ongoing growth and shareholder value creation in the year ahead.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Amphenol outlines $6.9B–$7B Q1 2026 sales target as CCS acquisition expands AI exposure
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Amphenol Corporation (APH) Q4 2025

Management View

  • CEO R. Norwitt highlighted a record-breaking fourth quarter, citing "sales and adjusted diluted earnings per share in the fourth quarter, both exceeding the high end of our guidance." He emphasized robust growth across nearly all end markets and pointed to a record $8.4 billion in orders, noting, "These robust orders were driven primarily by data center demand related in particular to artificial intelligence investments being planned by a number of our large customers."
  • Norwitt announced the closure of the Trexon acquisition, which expands Amphenol's defense market offerings, and confirmed the earlier-than-expected closing of the CommScope (CCS) acquisition, stating, "We expect CommScope to generate full year 2026 sales of $4.1 billion and to add $0.15 to Amphenol's 2026 adjusted earnings per share."
  • CFO Craig Lampo reported, "The company closed the fourth quarter of 2025 with record sales of $6.4 billion and GAAP and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.97 and $0.93, respectively." Lampo also highlighted a "very strong book-to-bill ratio of 1.31:1," driven by IT datacom market bookings, and discussed a robust liquidity position and the impact of the CCS acquisition on future interest expenses.

Outlook

  • Management guided Q1 2026 sales in the range of $6.9 billion to $7 billion and adjusted diluted EPS of $0.91 to $0.93. Norwitt stated, "This would represent significant sales growth from prior year of 43% to 45% and adjusted diluted EPS growth of 44% to 48%."
  • Guidance includes approximately $900 million in sales and $0.02 of adjusted EPS accretion from the CommScope acquisition.
  • Lampo indicated the adjusted tax rate of 25.5% will continue into 2026.

Financial Results

  • Fourth quarter sales were $6.4 billion, up 49% in U.S. dollars and 37% organically year-over-year, with sequential sales up 4%.
  • Adjusted diluted EPS reached $0.97, up 76% from the prior year. GAAP operating margin was 26.8%, with adjusted operating margin at 27.5%.
  • Record orders of $8.431 billion for the quarter led to a book-to-bill ratio of 1.31:1, primarily driven by IT datacom demand for AI applications.
  • Full-year sales were $23.1 billion, up 52%, and adjusted operating margin was 26.2%.
  • Segment performance: Communications Solutions sales grew 78%, Harsh Environment Solutions up 31%, Interconnect and Sensor Systems up 21% (all year-over-year).
  • Operating cash flow for the quarter was $1.7 billion, with free cash flow at $1.5 billion.
  • The company repurchased 1.3 million shares and returned $373 million to shareholders during the quarter.

Q&A

  • William Stein, Truist Securities: Asked about the nature and concentration of strong bookings and order duration. Norwitt explained, "These robust orders were driven primarily by data center demand related in particular to artificial intelligence investments... this is not because of kind of getting in line, so to speak... it's giving us the comfort through their own commitments to Amphenol that we should then make the commitments in capital."
  • Amit Daryanani, Evercore ISI: Asked about the breadth of offerings post-CCS deal and fiber optics exposure. Norwitt stated, "With CCS... CCS vaults us into a position of breadth and depth in the technology around fiber optic interconnect that is a real expansion of our capabilities."
  • Luke Junk, Baird: Inquired on integration steps for CommScope. Norwitt responded, "That word integration is not a word in the Amphenol lexicon... what we do talk about is letting them evolve into the Amphenol family, letting them be who they were because it's a fabulous organization."
  • Wamsi Mohan, BofA Securities: Asked about Q1 IT datacom organic outlook and AI program growth. Norwitt described broad-based customer engagement, indicating, "We have an enormous position with a lot of different customers up and down the stack of AI... their investment plans are all going up."
  • Additional analyst questions focused on order sustainability, margin outlook, CapEx trajectory, and specific end-market trends, with management emphasizing confidence in operational culture and future growth.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed strong interest in AI-driven growth, order momentum, and the impact of acquisitions, with questions probing sustainability, competitive positioning, and capital allocation. Tone was positive but probing for clarity on long-term order visibility and integration strategy.
  • Management maintained a confident and upbeat tone in prepared remarks, frequently citing "record" achievements and emphasizing operational agility. In Q&A, Norwitt and Lampo provided detailed explanations, often expressing pride and confidence, while occasionally clarifying on the nuances of integration and investment.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed increased enthusiasm, with a greater focus on AI, acquisition benefits, and operational scale.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for Q1 2026 sales ($6.9B–$7B) and EPS ($0.91–$0.93) represents a notable step up from Q4 2025 guidance. The CCS acquisition was closed earlier than anticipated and is expected to significantly impact sales and EPS.
  • Book-to-bill ratio surged to 1.31:1 (from 0.99:1 in Q3), reflecting record orders, especially in IT datacom/AI.
  • Segment growth rates accelerated in Communications Solutions and Harsh Environment Solutions compared to Q3.
  • Management tone grew more bullish, highlighting large-scale acquisition integration and robust order momentum.
  • Analysts' questions shifted toward order sustainability, future CapEx, and acquisition synergies versus the prior quarter's focus on margin incrementals and segment profitability.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management acknowledged a $100 million discrete tax accrual related to notices from Chinese tax authorities, with ongoing discussions on appropriate tax positions.
  • Higher adjusted tax rate (25.5%) due to income mix shift to higher-tax jurisdictions is expected to persist.
  • Potential margin headwinds from the integration of CCS, metals costs, and seasonality were discussed, but Lampo stated, "I wouldn't say that, that, at least as of now, we see having any significant impact on kind of our margin outlook as we're moving into '26."
  • Supply chain constraints, metals prices, and global geopolitical volatility were noted as ongoing management challenges, mitigated by the company's decentralized operating culture.

Final Takeaway

Amphenol closed 2025 with record sales and earnings, driven by broad-based demand and outstanding operational execution, especially in AI-related markets. The early completion of the CommScope acquisition significantly expands Amphenol's fiber optic and industrial portfolio, positioning the company for further growth in 2026. Management projects robust sales and EPS for Q1 2026, buoyed by record orders, a strong book-to-bill ratio, and continued momentum in key end markets, while remaining vigilant around tax, integration, and supply chain risks.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
